The most commonly reported sounds that trigger negative emotional responses such as annoyance, pain, or fear include:

  • Low-frequency sounds like drilling machines, traffic noise, and dogs barking
  • High-frequency sounds such as dishes rattling, crying children, applause, dentists drilling, and metal shopping carts
  • Broadband sounds from a TV, speech, alarms, and kitchen machines
  • Sudden sounds like hammering, a car horn, or a door slamming

Hyperacusis can be linked to hearing loss and tinnitus (ringing in the ear). Severe cases of reduced sound tolerance may lead to seizures and loss of balance.

What the average person considers normal sound levels can be screamingly loud to someone with reduced sound tolerance.

Most people have a hearing threshold of pain at 120dB, which is louder than a rock concert or a jackhammer. Below that, the loudness discomfort level is typically around 100dB, similar to the noise level when riding a motorcycle.

Those suffering from reduced sound tolerance often have a loudness discomfort level below 80dB, which is equivalent to someone shouting. This 20dB difference means the perceived loudness can be four times greater!

Can my earplugs help?

Studies indicate that overusing hearing protection may actually cause reduced sound tolerance and worsen an existing condition. Overprotection occurs when hearing protection isolates the user, contributing to worsening symptoms.

For instance, foam earplugs can completely block out surrounding noise.

This creates a vicious cycle where you unknowingly train your ear and brain to become more sensitive to sounds that are not damaging to your hearing. As this progresses, the threshold is lowered, making more sounds unbearable.

How is it treated?

In Norway, reduced sound tolerance is treated by audio therapists, with services covered by the National Health Service. Treatment typically involves counseling, sound training, and cognitive therapy, sometimes combined with physical aids like broadband noise generators.

A key insight in treatment is understanding that normal sound levels are not immediately harmful.

The treatment aims to retrain and recalibrate the connection between the ear and the brain.

This process usually takes months, and many individuals become dependent on their earplugs. Stopping the use of earplugs “cold turkey” can be very difficult and painful. Conventional earplugs often exacerbate the problem by reducing sounds in the higher frequency range.

This is typically where those with reduced sound tolerance face the most challenges. Minuendo variable earplugs offer an advantage in recovering from reduced sound tolerance.

Minuendo earplugs are part of the treatment, allowing for gradual opening over time. No other earplugs provide stepless variability while maintaining a natural sound experience (flat frequency response).

Reference: “Hyperacusis and Disorders of Sound Intolerance: Clinical and Research Perspectives” by Marc Fagelson (Author), David M. Baguley (Author)

This text has been validated and edited by Tina Trones, an audio therapist at Linderud Audiopedagosiske Senter in Oslo, Norway.

items.title: Bedroom home recording studio equipments for beginners
items.editorNotes: Ensure all links are functional and images are displayed correctly.
items.excerptBefore: A comprehensive guide for beginners looking to set up a bedroom home recording studio, including essential equipment like microphones, audio interfaces, and MIDI keyboards.
items.excerptAfter: A comprehensive guide for beginners looking to set up a bedroom home recording studio, covering essential equipment like microphones, audio interfaces, and MIDI keyboards.
items.seoTitleBefore: Bedroom Home Recording Studio Equipment for Beginners
items.seoTitleAfter: Essential Equipment for Your Bedroom Home Recording Studio
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Discover essential equipment for building your own bedroom home recording studio. From microphones to audio interfaces, find everything you need to start…
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover the must-have equipment for building your own bedroom home recording studio. From microphones to audio interfaces, find everything you need to…
items.beforeBody:
If you are looking to build your own bedroom home recording studio in 2022, we have the perfect guide for you. Go through this checklist of equipment you'll need and start building!

Here you will find all the gear you need to get started recording music right from the comfort of your bedroom.

Wires and cables

XLR cables

You may have a high-end condenser and a good quality audio interface, but with a worn-out XLR cable, you risk having noise in your recording. Mechanical damage, corrosion, and dry-out cracking can happen easily if you buy a cheap XLR cable.

We recommend Mogami Gold Studio - you can pick between 3, 6, 15, and 25-foot lengths.

TS vs TRS cables

So what is the difference between the two and which one do you need? TS cable connectors are perfect for instruments with mono signals, like guitars, mixers, or a receiver.

TRS cables have three contact points. They are commonly used to connect balanced equipment or for running both left and right mono signals to stereo headphones.

Microphones

Shure SM57 Dynamic microphone

The Shure SM57 is one of the most legendary recording microphones on the market. Many award-winning albums have been recorded with just a single SM57, making this the best choice for a versatile mic that can do everything.

The SM57 is best suited for instruments but can also work well with vocals. It provides a very neutral sound, keeping the voice clean and clear without pops. To enhance clarity, consider using a mic stand and a pop filter.

  • Perfect price range for the quality of sound
  • High loudness tolerance
  • Die-cast steel construction

Smartphone Studio Mic

Looking for a no-nonsense high-quality audio mic? Look no further. This Smartphone Studio Mic has the easiest setup among the mics we've tested. Just plug it into your device and start recording. It works with iOS, Android, macOS, and Windows, recording with studio quality of up to 24-96kHz.

  • No setup hassle
  • No battery to charge
  • High-performance audio quality with 20Hz - 20kHz frequency response
  • Direct monitoring with integrated gain control and headphone output

RODE NT1-A

If you want an incredible dynamic range, this is the all-around mic for you. You can mic up your drums or even a quiet talking voice, and the RODE NT1-A will give you a clear, quiet, and clean sound. It comes with a one-year warranty that can be extended to ten years upon registration.

  • Includes a RODE SM6 shock mount, pop filter, and dust cover
  • Excellent dynamic range
  • Reduces hiss from grounded mics
  • Comes with a full microphone setup

Audio interface

Audio interfaces allow you to connect and play with professional mics and instruments, making them an essential component of your home studio.

Scarlett USB interface

  • Offers great value for money
  • Several versions available depending on the number of inputs you need
  • Easy to set up

Our top recommendation is the Focusrite Scarlett Studio. If you want to set up, record, and play back within five minutes, this is the best option. It simply does what it's supposed to do without fail.

It also comes with a range of software: Avid Pro Tools, Ableton, Softube, and even a three-month Splice Sounds subscription.

If you choose the Scarlett 2i2 Studio, the interface includes a CM25 MKIII condenser microphone and HP60 MkIII headphones.

DAW

Are you looking for the best value DAW subscriptions or even free ones? We’ve covered almost all DAW software available! Check out our in-depth review for DAW tools here.

Great budget monitor

If you are serious about creating quality recordings, mixing, or mastering, having good monitors is a perfect addition to your bedroom home recording studio equipment.

Adam Audio T5V

This is a highly affordable two-way monitor, perfect for smaller rooms or a home studio. It features top-end EQ adjustment and is very customizable. It also allows for both balanced and unbalanced connections.

Yamaha HS5

This 5” woofer is ideal for a bedroom home recording studio. With the HS5, Yamaha caters to both professionals and beginners while keeping it affordable. It has excellent build quality and a great frequency response, allowing for effective mixing and mastering. Its clean and refined design adds a professional feel to your studio space.

MIDI Keyboard

Arturia MiniLab Mk II

At first glance, the MiniLab MKII showcases sturdy and high-quality keys, knobs, pads, pitch, and modulation controls. Some parts of the case are made from metal, giving it a solid feel. It also comes with hundreds of free virtual instruments that you can download. Ableton Lite host software is included, but it is limited to creating a maximum of eight tracks.

Genki Wave MIDI Ring

This unique gestural MIDI ring controller is our top pick for saving space in your bedroom. You can wear the Genki Wave on your finger and use natural gestures to control sound. This MIDI controller recognizes six gestures: Tilt, Pan, Roll, Vibrato, Tap, and Click.

For more information, read more about MIDI controllers.

items.title: Top 10 drum accessories and tools every drummer needs to have
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original factual content.
items.excerptBefore: Discover the top 10 essential drum accessories and tools every drummer needs to enhance their performance and protect their gear.
items.excerptAfter: Discover the top 10 essential drum accessories every drummer needs to enhance their skills and protect their gear.
items.seoTitleBefore: Top 10 Drum Accessories and Tools Every Drummer Needs
items.seoTitleAfter: Top 10 Drum Accessories Every Drummer Needs
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Explore the must-have drum accessories and tools that every drummer should own to improve their skills and protect their equipment.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore the must-have drum accessories and tools that every drummer should own to improve their skills and protect their equipment.
items.beforeBody:
Whether you’re a beginner drummer or a seasoned professional, having the right accessories and tools is essential. These trusty items protect your drums from the rigors of travel and help you improve your drumming skills.

So, what are the top 10 must-have drum accessories? Let's dive in and discover the items you need to keep your beat intact.

1. Soundbrenner's wearable metronomes

Core 2 Core Steel Pulse

The Soundbrenner Core 2 and Pulse vibrating metronomes are not just metronomes; they are comprehensive tools that every drummer should have. They replace traditional audio clicks with tactile vibrations, enhancing focus and rhythmic precision. These devices offer adjustable features, including BPM, time signatures, and subdivisions, making practice sessions more effective.

They come with an app that allows you to save rhythm presets and build set lists. An integrated dB meter provides real-time volume monitoring to protect your hearing. With multi-day battery life and splash resistance, these metronomes are ideal for both short and long practice sessions.

2. Drum shell protection

Buy now

Protecting your drum shells is crucial. Invest in padded drum bags or hard-shell cases for optimal protection. Soft cases are great for local gigs, while hard cases provide superior protection for long travels. These are essential drum accessories you cannot overlook.

3. Minuendo lossless earplugs

Buy now

Minuendo lossless earplugs are essential for any drummer. They are the world's first variable passive Hi-Fi earplugs, featuring patented membrane technology that provides a natural, clear sound without frequency degradation.

The earplugs offer stepless adjustments from -7dB to -25dB, allowing you to find your ideal acoustic balance. With a magnetic locking system and various ear tip options, these earplugs are versatile and secure for any musical setting.

4. Cymbal bag

Buy now

A dedicated cymbal bag is a must for protecting your delicate cymbals. Ensure it has dividing sections to prevent them from clashing and causing cracks or dents.

5. Hardware bag

Buy now

Your drum hardware is the backbone of your kit and deserves its own protective space. A durable hardware bag with compartments will keep your stands, pedals, and other hardware organized and secure.

6. Snare drum case

Buy now

Your snare drum is the heart of your kit and deserves special attention. A padded snare drum case is essential to ensure your snare remains in top condition and ready to produce that crisp sound every time.

7. Drum key

Buy now

Imagine going to a gig and realizing you can't tune your drums. A lost key can be a nightmare. Keep a spare key in your drum case at all times.

8. Spare drumheads

Buy now

Drumheads wear out over time, and having a spare set can be a lifesaver during a performance. Ensure they are properly protected to prevent damage.

9. Drum rug or mat

Buy now

Stability is crucial when playing the drums. A rug or mat keeps your kit in place and protects floors from scratches and damage.

10. Maintenance kit

Buy now

A maintenance kit is essential. Include a small toolkit with items like drum head tape, tension rods, and a cleaner. This kit will save the day when you need quick fixes on the go.

Wrapping up!

With this selection of accessories, you’re ready to take on any musical venture with your drum set! Handle them with care and replace them as needed to maintain their effectiveness.

While it may not count as an accessory, mastering rhythm is crucial. We cover the basics of rhythm and how to develop a solid understanding in these articles, so give them a read!

If you are a beginner looking to master this amazing instrument, check out our blog for expert tips on learning drums.

items.title: What is a MIDI keyboard and what is it used for?
Alternative Rock: Evolution of Seattle's distinctive sound
items.editorNotes: Ensure all links are functional and images are correctly displayed.
items.excerptBefore: Explore the evolution of Seattle's alternative rock scene, its key bands, and the cultural influences that shaped this distinctive sound.
items.excerptAfter: Explore the evolution of Seattle's alternative rock scene, its key bands, and the cultural influences that shaped a generation.
items.seoTitleBefore: Alternative Rock: Evolution of Seattle's Distinctive Sound
items.seoTitleAfter: The Evolution of Seattle's Alternative Rock Scene
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Discover the journey of Seattle's alternative rock scene, its influential bands, and the cultural backdrop that contributed to its unique sound.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover how Seattle's unique cultural landscape and key bands like Nirvana and Pearl Jam shaped the evolution of alternative rock.
items.beforeBody:
Picture Seattle—a city bustling with innovation and artistry, a place where little-known bands rocked dimly lit venues and transformed underground culture. It's within this vibrant tapestry that alternative rock, also known as grunge, found its unmistakable voice.

Breaking away from the polished, overproduced tracks flooding the mainstream, Seattle's contribution was authentic, raw, and deeply emotive. This wasn't merely an addition to the rock and roll canon; it was a significant upheaval that compelled global audiences to sit up, listen, and reevaluate what they knew about the genre.

The confluence of influences

Seattle's alternative rock scene didn't emerge in a vacuum; it was shaped by a rich mix of music traditions, from punk and metal to folk.

Garage rock and punk provided the initial scaffolding for the edifice that would become alternative rock. Originating in the basements of rebellious youth, these styles carried anti-establishment themes that seethed against conformity. Their soundscapes were often simple—three-chord structures and an ethos of raw energy.

Then came grunge, like a roaring tide from the Pacific Northwest. A gritty sub-genre that held the banner high for the complexity and thematic depth alternative rock could offer. Grunge was the musical embodiment of Seattle's rainy skies, its flannel-clad youth, and its isolated, almost introspective cultural heartbeat.

It would be a disservice to discuss the "Seattle Sound" without touching upon the city's unique characteristics. Demographically, Seattle was far from the star-studded glam of Los Angeles. This was a blue-collar city, reeling from economic downturns and beset by the grunge of industrial labor. The city's youth, faced with the dreariness of dead-end jobs and societal discontent, found an outlet in the burgeoning alternative rock scene.

Key alternative rock bands and their lasting impact

In any vibrant music scene, there are bands that stand as pillars, defining the movement. Seattle's alternative rock scene was no exception.

Nirvana

Source: Pinterest

Exploring the history of Nirvana reveals that "Smells Like Teen Spirit" was far more than a hit. It was a call to arms for a generation looking for something real, embodying the distinct sounds of alternative rock. The song shook the foundations of mainstream music and brought Kurt Cobain, who was never comfortable with fame, into the spotlight. His deep and honest lyrics resonated with many, touching on complicated emotions and capturing the spirit of the era.

Pearl Jam

Source: Pearl Jam

Pearl Jam blended the grunge style with classic alternative rock elements. They didn't stop at music; they used their platform for activism, supporting causes that were important to them. Their ongoing influence proves their importance in this unique music scene.

Soundgarden and Alice in Chains

Source: RollingStone

These bands brought a heavier touch to the grunge atmosphere, mixing in influences from metal and tackling darker, more complex themes. Their music broadened the emotional and thematic range of what grunge could offer.

Seattle's music scene: A hotbed for creativity

Seattle stood apart as a pillar for musical innovation, partly due to its isolation from other music hubs like Los Angeles or Nashville. This allowed the city to create a different sound, unaffected by outside influences. It may not have the world’s best music venues, but underground locations thrived. They gave garage bands the chance to become local legends and ultimately shape the alternative rock landscape.

The city's unique blend of punk, metal, and folk influences added to a genre that was far more than a sum of its parts. Bands like Nirvana and Pearl Jam tapped into complex emotions and themes, resonating with a generation in search of authenticity.

Seattle's weather—those long, introspective rainy days—also played a role in shaping the music. The sound that formed wasn't just another genre; it was a manifestation of a specific time and place that left a lasting imprint on alternative rock's evolution.

Wrapping up

Seattle's indelible impact on alternative rock serves as a reminder of the transformative power of local music scenes. This wave of creativity didn't just capture the spirit of a generation; it redefined what rock could be, forging new paths for artists to come. It is proof of the strong potential of places and communities to ignite groundbreaking cultural movements, showing that the next seismic shift in music could emerge from anywhere.

items.title: Weird instruments: Our top 6 picks in the world of music
items.editorNotes: Ensure all links and images are correctly referenced and functional.
items.excerptBefore: Unveiling the captivating world of weird instruments that challenge conventional music, adding a unique twist to auditory experiences.
items.excerptAfter: Explore our top 6 picks of weird instruments that redefine music with their unique sounds and designs.
items.seoTitleBefore: Weird instruments: Our top 6 picks in the world of music
items.seoTitleAfter: Weird Instruments: Our Top 6 Picks in the World of Music
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Unveiling the captivating world of weird instruments that challenge conventional music, adding a unique twist to auditory experiences.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover the top 6 weird instruments that add a unique twist to music. From the Hurdy-Gurdy to the Badgermin, explore these sonic oddities.
items.beforeBody:
Imagine a world where the rhythm isn't just carried by drums and guitars, but by instruments so bizarre that they redefine the very essence of music. Today, we embark on a journey into the realm of sonic oddities - the top 5 weird instruments that add a refreshing and unconventional twist to the symphony of sounds.

1. Cross-Grainger Kangaroo-Pouch Tone-Tool

Source: Omeka

Originating from the curious minds of Percy Grainger and Burnett Cross, this weird instrument might leave you scratching your head initially. It gets its quirky name from its appearance, which is like the pouch of a kangaroo. Musicians manipulate oscillations by shuffling rolls of perforated paper, which might remind you of an old-school computer. 

An amazing fusion of mechanical and musical genius, this instrument is proof of the saying - "Innovation knows no bounds."

Learn more

2. Playtron

In the realm of weird instruments, the Playtron by Soundbrenner stands out. This remarkable device has the uncanny ability to convert everyday objects into MIDI instruments using alligator clips. From fruits to metal, up to 16 conductive items can be turned into musical wonders with sub-millisecond latency, ensuring instant music creation.

Designed for all musicians, it's light, durable construction means you can create tunes on the go—whether you're in the studio or surrounded by nature. All it takes is a connection to your device and your favorite music app.

Buy now

3. The hurdy-gurdy: Medieval roots with endless whimsy

Source: The Metropolitan Museum of Art

The next weird instrument on our list takes us back in time to the medieval era with the Hurdy-Gurdy. Combining the elements of a violin and a mechanical crank, this instrument produces an enchanting blend of traditional and innovative sounds. 

As the crank is turned, a wheel rubs against the strings, creating melodies that evoke images of old-world landscapes and timeless tales.

Buy now

4. Badgermin

Source: Nervous Squirrel

Hold onto your seats because this one's truly wild! Imagine combining a badger with a theremin, and what do you get? A Badgermin! A true testament to the bizarre, the Badgermin produces ethereal electronic tunes while preserving the outward appearance of a badger. This weird instrument is a homage to the world of electronic music and the natural world.

Learn more

5. The Otamatone: Quirky musical note-playing gadget

Source: Otamatone.jp

Transitioning to the whimsical, we encounter the Otamatone. Resembling a musical note with a face, this quirky and weird instrument is played by sliding a finger along its stem while squeezing its cheeks to control the pitch. 

Emitting sounds that range from humorous to surprisingly melodic, the Otamatone is a delightful fusion of toy and instrument that captures the hearts of both children and adults.

Buy now

6. Hornucopian Dronepipe

Source: 3D Today

Last on our list of weird instruments is the avant-garde world of the Hornucopian Dronepipe. Now, this might seem straight out of a steampunk movie, but rest assured, its unusual looks match its otherworldly sounds. This 3D-printed piece, though a modern marvel, carries an air of ancient tribal calls.

Learn more

Bottom line

In the vast ocean of musical instruments, it's the unusual ones that often surprise us the most. Our top 6 picks barely scratch the surface of what's out there, yet they show the innovative spirit of music creation.

For those diving into mastering these quirky instruments, we recommend the Soundbrenner Core 2 a tool designed to perfect your rhythm and elevate your playing.

If you liked this blog, don't forget to explore our blog on unique instruments. Who knows what other music wonders await? Happy playing!
items.afterBody:
Imagine a world where rhythm isn't just carried by drums and guitars, but by instruments so bizarre that they redefine the very essence of music. Today, we embark on a journey into the realm of sonic oddities - the top 6 weird instruments that add a refreshing and unconventional twist to the symphony of sounds.

1. Cross-Grainger Kangaroo-Pouch Tone-Tool

Source: Omeka

Originating from the curious minds of Percy Grainger and Burnett Cross, this weird instrument might leave you scratching your head initially. It gets its quirky name from its appearance, which resembles the pouch of a kangaroo. Musicians manipulate oscillations by shuffling rolls of perforated paper, reminiscent of an old-school computer.

An amazing fusion of mechanical and musical genius, this instrument is proof of the saying - "Innovation knows no bounds."

2. Playtron

In the realm of weird instruments, the Playtron by Soundbrenner stands out. This remarkable device can convert everyday objects into MIDI instruments using alligator clips. From fruits to metal, up to 16 conductive items can be turned into musical wonders with sub-millisecond latency, ensuring instant music creation.

Designed for all musicians, its light and durable construction means you can create tunes on the go—whether you're in the studio or surrounded by nature. All it takes is a connection to your device and your favorite music app.

3. The hurdy-gurdy: Medieval roots with endless whimsy

Source: The Metropolitan Museum of Art

The next weird instrument on our list takes us back to the medieval era with the Hurdy-Gurdy. Combining elements of a violin and a mechanical crank, this instrument produces an enchanting blend of traditional and innovative sounds.

As the crank is turned, a wheel rubs against the strings, creating melodies that evoke images of old-world landscapes and timeless tales.

items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore our curated list of the top 10 gifts for saxophone players in 2025, featuring essential gadgets and unique accessories to delight any musician.
items.beforeBody:
Have a saxophone enthusiast in your life and wondering what to surprise them with this year? While they might not be hitting the spot as well as George Michael in his song “Careless Whisper” just yet, however, here are the 10 best gifts for saxophone players that are guaranteed to!

Our top 10 best gifts for saxophone players:

From the latest musical gadgets to saxophone-shaped cuff links. Here is our list of best gifts for saxophone players has something for every saxophone lover out there:

1. Soundbrenner Core 2

This device is the ultimate practice companion for saxophone players. The Soundbrenner Core 2 combines a vibrating metronome, practice tracker, chromatic tuner, decibel meter, and smartwatch into one sleek gadget. It makes practicing not just effective but also enjoyable, ensuring musicians stay on beat and in tune. A top gift for any saxophonist looking to elevate their skills!

 

Buy now

2. Flow microphone

The Soundbrenner Flow microphone records high-quality sound wherever you go. This versatile tool connects effortlessly to iOS and Android devices, as well as Windows and Mac computers, without needing extra apps or drivers. Its cardioid polar pattern minimizes background noise, ensuring crystal-clear audio capture. 

Buy now

3. Legere saxophone reeds

With all the options of reeds now available online, it's near impossible to choose just one. So why not gift a couple of options and make the decision a little easier? Besides, Legere reeds come in multiple strengths and are considered some of the best out there.  

Buy now

4. Minuendo lossless earplugs

Hearing protection is a must for all musicians. Minuendo lossless earplugs come with eleven pairs of ear tips. This pair has features such as step-less adjustment, a variable passive Hi-Fi filter, and guaranteeing zero sound leak. 

Buy now

5. MRCUFF Saxophone cufflinks

These MRCUFF cufflinks make the perfect gift for the saxophone player that has it all! Classy enough to be worn to formal occasions to add a touch of personality, and fun enough to be sported every day. An additional perk, you don't have to worry about damage during transit! These are shipped in a hard-bound box for additional safety.

Buy now

6. Digital effects pedal

Move over, guitarists! The MXR M300 Digital reverb effects pedal is one of the best gifts for saxophone players looking to add that extra oomph effect to their next performance. Unlike most other pedal models available, this one is not re-purposed for saxophone sound and is designed to let that sax effect truly ring out.  

Buy now 

7. Reed case

Are those reeds rattling around in that saxophone case? Give them a home with this sleek spring-enclosure reed case designed specifically for saxophone players.  

Buy now 

8. Eison Saxophone Stand

It's foldable, portable, and the perfect gift for any saxophone player to show them just how much you appreciate their craft! Also, No more backache for them means extra brownie points for you. 

Buy now

9. Core body strap

Does that special saxophone player in your life already own the Core Metronome? Then, gift them something you already know they’ll appreciate. This replacement strap is the perfect gift for that well-loved Core or Core Steel.  

Buy now

10. Protec MX304CT Saxophone Case

Is that saxophone carrying bag looking a little worst for wear lately? Then it is pretty obvious that it is time for a replacement! The lightweight shock-resistant foam casing and padded shoulder straps mean extra comfort for the saxophone and the player.

Surprise your loved one with the latest Protec Saxophone Contoured MAX Case, because we guarantee you'll be their favorite person at the party!  

Buy now

items.title: Top 8 best MIDI keyboards for live performances and musicians in 2025
Best gifts for piano players 2025
items.editorNotes: The article has been rewritten for clarity and readability while preserving all factual information and required links.
items.excerptBefore: Looking for the perfect gift for a pianist? Discover the best gifts for piano players in 2025, from innovative metronomes and in-ear monitors to essential sheet music and studio gear. Find inspiring ideas for every piano lover - whether they’re a beginner or a seasoned musician!
items.excerptAfter: Discover the best gifts for piano players in 2025, from innovative metronomes to immersive sound equipment.
items.seoTitleAfter: Best Gifts for Piano Players 2025
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Discover the 10 best gifts for piano players in 2025, from metronomes and in-ear monitors to must-have sheet music and studio gear.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore our top picks for piano player gifts in 2025, including the Soundbrenner Pulse, Wave in-ear monitors, and more!
items.beforeBody:
Searching for the perfect gift for a piano player? Whether your loved one is just starting out or already a seasoned pianist, our updated guide features the best gifts for piano players in 2025! Each gift is thoughtfully selected to inspire creativity, enhance performance, and bring more joy to piano practice. Read on for our top picks!

1. Soundbrenner Pulse/Core 2 – the ultimate wearable metronome

Mastering rhythm is essential for every pianist, but traditional audible metronomes can be distracting. The Soundbrenner Pulse/Core 2 revolutionizes piano practice by providing silent, tactile beats you can feel on your wrist or arm. Perfect for all skill levels, this wearable vibrating metronome helps players develop precise timing and musical confidence, making it our top choice for piano gifts in 2025.

Buy now

2. Wave in-ear monitors – immersive sound for pianists

Upgrade any pianist’s listening experience with Wave in-ear monitors. Designed for musicians, audiophiles, and music lovers alike, Wave features a 10mm dynamic driver for stunning sound clarity and deep bass. With up to 36dB passive noise isolation and six different ear tips, pianists can focus on every nuance of their music—on stage, in the studio, or during practice at home. Durable, comfortable, and travel-ready, Wave is the perfect gift for players who value pristine audio.

Buy now

3. Akai Professional MPK Mini MK3 – best MIDI keyboard for piano players

Looking for a compact MIDI keyboard controller? The Akai MPK Mini MK3 is a studio essential for pianists who compose, perform, or record music. It features responsive keys, drum pads, and comes with powerful synth packages. Fully compatible with both Windows and macOS, it’s a versatile tool for any digital audio workstation (DAW).

Buy now

4. Behringer Ultratone K900FX – versatile keyboard amp

The Behringer Ultratone K900FX is a powerhouse amp for keyboards and digital pianos. With multiple channels, XLR input, and a 24-bit digital FX processor featuring 100 effects, it delivers professional-grade sound for gigs or home practice. Perfect for pianists who want to amplify their performances with clarity and versatility.

Buy now

5. Metal music book clip and page holder

Sometimes the simplest gifts are the most useful. This high-grade stainless steel music book clip keeps sheet music perfectly in place, even during intense piano practice. Its durable construction ensures it won’t bend or rust, making it a thoughtful stocking stuffer for any pianist.

Buy now

6. Arturia Sound Explorers Collection 2 – premium software suite

This exclusive suite gives music creators access to a wide range of Arturia’s virtual instruments and effects. With thousands of professional-grade presets, the Sound Explorers Collection 2 empowers pianists to compose, record, and experiment with new sounds.

Buy now

7. JBL 305P MkII – 5-Inch powered studio monitor

Clear, accurate sound is critical for mixing and recording. The JBL 305P MkII studio monitor offers outstanding audio fidelity with impressive bass response—ideal for pianists who record or produce music. Say goodbye to headphone fatigue and enjoy your mix as it was meant to be heard.

Buy now

8. Behringer Xenyx 802S – compact studio mixer

Need a portable mixer for your home studio or band? The Behringer Xenyx 802S delivers professional mixing features in a small footprint, including two XLR mic inputs, three-band EQ, and ultra-low noise operation. Great for piano players setting up their first home recording setup.

Buy now

9. The Library of Piano Classics – sheet music book

For pianists who love to explore the great works, this collection features over 100 timeless pieces, from Schubert’s “Moment Musical” to Chopin’s “Minute Waltz” and Beethoven’s “Rondo a Capriccio.” There’s a rewarding mix of beginner and advanced pieces to inspire every player.

Buy now

10. The Library of Disney Songs – piano songbook

Bring some Disney magic to your piano bench! This collection includes the most beloved Disney classics, with arrangements that are true to the originals. It’s a fantastic gift for pianists of all ages who want to relive their favorite movie moments through music.

Buy now

Whether you’re shopping for a student, a performer, or a casual piano enthusiast, these thoughtful gifts for piano players are guaranteed to make any music lover’s day. Happy gifting!

items.title: Practice tracking now on The Metronome by Soundbrenner App
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining factual accuracy.
items.excerptBefore: Discover the latest update to The Metronome by Soundbrenner app, featuring practice tracking, customizable goals, and an all-new home page design.
items.excerptAfter: Discover the latest features in The Metronome by Soundbrenner app, including practice tracking, personalized goals, and an updated home page design.
items.seoTitleBefore: Practice Tracking Now Available on The Metronome by…
items.seoTitleAfter: Practice Tracking Now Available in The Metronome by…
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Explore the new practice tracking feature in The Metronome by Soundbrenner app, including goal setting, session planning, and more!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore the new practice tracking features in The Metronome by Soundbrenner app, including goal setting, session planning, and an all-new home page design.
items.beforeBody:
Musicians, a massive update has just landed on our practice tracking app, The Metronome by Soundbrenner (version 1.31.0)! We’re super excited about this, and we hope you are too. This feature is currently available for iOS only, but Android will launch soon!

What's new in The Metronome by Soundbrenner app?

Practice tracking

Set goals

  • Planning to practice for 10 minutes? 1 hour? You can now set your practice goals according to whatever you want.

Add your instrument

  • Choose from our list of instruments, or add your own!

Bonus:

  • Soundbrenner Plus subscribers can add personalized instruments (including custom photos), tag your instruments when practicing, and get separate statistics that you can share with your friends!

Session plan

  • Set specific goals for each practice session to help you make faster improvements.

Timer ⏱

  • After you start your practice session, a timer will begin recording how long you’ve been practicing!

End-of-session recap

  • You can now note down your entire practice recap! How did you feel? Did you achieve your goal? What could you do better next time? Reflecting on your practice session helps you make faster progress.

Streaks 🔥

  • Humans are creatures of habit. We love keeping streaks like personal high scores. You can now see how many days in a row you’ve practiced and even share it with your friends!

All new home page design

We’ve got an all-new home section designed to give you a truly personalized experience to motivate your practice habits!

Streak goals

  • Not everyone has the time to practice daily, but we all want to keep our streaks up! You can change your streak goals so you can practice at your own pace.

Practice stats

  • There’s something really satisfying about seeing your personal records. You can also view your practice sessions and personal high scores in this section. You can share these with your friends on social media! 😉

Automatic practice sessions

If starting a new practice session each time is too much of a hassle for you, you can toggle on the “Automatic practice sessions” button. This will record a new practice session for you every time you start using the Soundbrenner metronome.

This is particularly convenient if you own a Soundbrenner wearable! All you'll have to do is tap and play 🎶

Want to request a feature?

Our team worked really hard to implement all these new features, so we hope that you enjoy this update to our practice tracking app and take your time to explore all the new details!

What would you like us to add to The Metronome by Soundbrenner app? Visit the Soundbrenner Suggestions page and let us know!

items.title: 8 drum accessories you need to become a master drummer
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while preserving all original links and image URLs.
items.excerptBefore: To become a master drummer, you'll need essential drum accessories like a quality drum set, tools, books, racks, bags, and other key items.
items.excerptAfter: Discover essential drum accessories that can elevate your drumming skills, whether you're an amateur or a professional. From drum kits to practice pads, find out what you need to become a master drummer.
items.seoTitleAfter: 8 Drum Accessories You Need to Become a Master Drummer
items.seoDescriptionBefore: To become a master drummer, you'll need essential drum accessories like a quality drum set, tools, books, racks, bags, and other key items.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore the essential drum accessories that every drummer needs to enhance their skills and performance. From drum kits to practice pads, learn what it…
items.beforeBody:
By Alice Mackenzie

There are far more amateur drummers in the world than professionals. The distance between moving from one to the other is quite large and requires lots of practice. However, if you intend to become a master drummer, you will need the right drum accessories.

Also, if you already play professionally, the kit will help improve your playing. Drumming necessity includes quality drum set, drum tool, books, racks, bags, and other necessary accessories.

Drum Kit

Every drummer needs drum accessories like a drum kit. The right drum kit should showcase your ability and music style while allowing you to have fun. You could start with a five-piece drum kit and add to it as your drumming gets better. This set will comprise a bass drum, rack toms, floor tom, and snare drum. Also, be ready to invest in quality drum heads that will take all the beating.

Drum Tool

Since you never know what might go wrong with your drum kit, having an all-in-one tool for adjustments and repairs is necessary.

Drum multi-tools come in all sizes and can contain loads of essential tools you never knew you needed. The best thing about a drum tool is that it is small to keep handy, but not so little that it ends up missing when needed. 

This CruzTools multi-tool contains a drum key, Allen keys, screwdrivers, and wrenches that you will require at one point or the other. It is a maintenance kit with a collection of heat-treated S2 alloy tools in a polished chrome finish. The drum key helps you tune your drum heads and make them sound better. Two wrenches are present to take care of the bass drum pedals and other hardware that need maintenance.

Drum Books

If you’ve ever tried mastering your own tracks, you know how difficult it is to get everything up to the right volume. It takes a lot of effort to make sure a song is loud enough that listeners don’t have to keep turning up the volume, but How are you supposed to be a pro if you do not improve your drumming?

There are several drum books written to teach drummers how they can strengthen their hand and foot techniques. You can also get lessons on stick control, learn how to play different drumming styles, and better know your drum rudiments.

Our recommendation:

  • Anatomy of Drumming (John Lamb)
  • Advanced Techniques for the Modern Drummer (Jim Chapin)
  • Progressive Steps to Syncopation for the Modern Drummer (Ted Reed Publications)

Drum Rack

A drum rack is a mounting system that grants you easy access when you play different instruments. As you develop your drumming skills, you start adding new appliances. These percussion pieces, which might include rack toms and cymbals, need proper arrangement to enhance your playing, and drum accessories like a drum rack can make this possible. It replaces the need to have different stands occupying space.

What makes a good drum rack:

  • Sturdy
  • Memory lock 
  • Material slip-proof

Cymbals

Quality cymbals will increase the joy you derive in your drumming. As your drumming proficiency increases, you will find the need to upgrade the sound of your cymbals. Get hi-hat, crash, or ride cymbal packs made for the type of music style you play. Note that the alloy material used to make the cymbal will determine how it plays.

If you are unfamiliar with cymbals, you are better off getting a new pack from one of the top cymbal makers: Paiste, Zildjian, Sabian, and Meinl. If you use an electronic drum set, you can expand your setup with these e-cymbals from drum-tec works.

Drumsticks

Drum accessories like drumsticks are essential, and you can never have enough of them. Durable wooden or aluminum materials are used to make drumsticks, and even the type of wood used impacts your drumming more than you know. The best one for you depends on your budget and how hard you play. Look at the thickness, the taper length, and the tip before choosing, no matter your music style. If you’re a jazz drummer, you may prefer a lighter stick, while rock and metal drummers will be more comfortable with a heavier one.

Drum Bags

As a master drummer, you need drum accessories like drum bags to keep your drums from damage. They are great, especially if you have to travel with your drums for rehearsals and shows. You could get a bag for each piece of your drum kit or have a big drum case where everything fits in nicely. The drum bag can also be an excellent place to keep your drumsticks and books while on the road.

Practice drums

Instead of banging away at your very expensive drum set at every point or constituting a level of noise with it, you can use practice pads to rehearse your drumming skills. The advantage of practice pads is that you practice silently, which improves your accuracy and speed significantly.

One can also use electronic drum sets as practice pads, which are essential drum accessories. The volume buttons aid in sound control, making them less loud. You can always plug in a headphone and be in a world of your own. Virtual drumming is another vital consideration, with Freedrum making it possible for you to learn new techniques without waking your neighbors. Of the free synth plugins out there, Helm easily has the best GUI. On top of that, it is an excellent software synthesizer that comes packed with features.

Metronome

Do you feel like rhythm changes at times? It's fast when you're excited, or sometimes it feels dragged. You can fix it by working on your natural sense of timing by using a metronome. At Soundbrenner, we create metronomes but instead of auditory cues, we use tactile cues like vibration to keep you on a beat.
Check out our shop to find out more about our vibrating metronomes.
items.afterBody:
By Alice Mackenzie

There are far more amateur drummers in the world than professionals. The distance between moving from one to the other is quite large and requires lots of practice. However, if you intend to become a master drummer, you will need the right drum accessories.

If you already play professionally, these accessories can help improve your playing. Essential drumming gear includes a quality drum set, drum tools, books, racks, bags, and other necessary accessories.

Drum kit

Every drummer needs a drum kit. The right drum kit should showcase your ability and music style while allowing you to have fun. You could start with a five-piece drum kit and add to it as your drumming improves. This set typically comprises a bass drum, rack toms, floor tom, and snare drum. Be prepared to invest in quality drum heads that can withstand heavy use.

Drum tool

Since you never know what might go wrong with your drum kit, having an all-in-one tool for adjustments and repairs is necessary.

Drum multi-tools come in various sizes and can contain essential tools you never knew you needed. The best thing about a drum tool is that it is compact enough to keep handy but not so small that it gets lost when needed.

This CruzTools multi-tool contains a drum key, Allen keys, screwdrivers, and wrenches that you will require at some point. It is a maintenance kit with a collection of heat-treated S2 alloy tools in a polished chrome finish. The drum key helps you tune your drum heads for better sound. Two wrenches are included for maintaining bass drum pedals and other hardware.

Drum books

If you’ve ever tried mastering your own tracks, you know how difficult it is to get everything up to the right volume. It takes a lot of effort to ensure a song is loud enough that listeners don’t have to keep turning up the volume. How are you supposed to be a pro if you do not improve your drumming?

Several drum books are available to teach drummers how to strengthen their hand and foot techniques. You can also find lessons on stick control, learn different drumming styles, and better understand your drum rudiments.

Our recommendations include:

  • Anatomy of Drumming (John Lamb)
  • Advanced Techniques for the Modern Drummer (Jim Chapin)
  • Progressive Steps to Syncopation for the Modern Drummer (Ted Reed Publications)

Drum rack

A drum rack is a mounting system that grants you easy access when playing different instruments. As you develop your drumming skills, you will start adding new pieces. These percussion instruments, which may include rack toms and cymbals, need proper arrangement to enhance your playing, and a drum rack can facilitate this. It replaces the need for multiple stands occupying space.

What makes a good drum rack:

  • Sturdy
  • Memory lock
  • Slip-proof material

Cymbals

Quality cymbals will enhance your drumming experience. As your proficiency increases, you will find the need to upgrade your cymbals. Get hi-hat, crash, or ride cymbal packs tailored to your music style. Note that the alloy material used to make the cymbal will determine its sound.

If you are unfamiliar with cymbals, consider getting a new pack from top manufacturers: Paiste, Zildjian, Sabian, and Meinl. If you use an electronic drum set, you can expand your setup with e-cymbals from drum-tec.

Drumsticks

Drumsticks are essential accessories, and you can never have enough of them. They are made from durable wood or aluminum, and the type of wood used can significantly impact your drumming. The best choice depends on your budget and playing style. Consider the thickness, taper length, and tip before choosing. Jazz drummers may prefer lighter sticks, while rock and metal drummers often opt for heavier ones.

Drum bags

As a master drummer, you need drum bags to protect your drums from damage. They are especially useful if you travel with your drums for rehearsals and shows. You can get a bag for each piece of your drum kit or a large drum case that fits everything nicely. Drum bags can also serve as excellent storage for your drumsticks and books while on the road.

Practice drums

Instead of banging away at your expensive drum set constantly, consider using practice pads to rehearse your skills. Practice pads allow you to practice silently, significantly improving your accuracy and speed.

You can also use electronic drum sets as practice pads, which are essential drum accessories. The volume controls help manage sound levels, and you can plug in headphones for a more personal experience. Virtual drumming is another valuable option, with Freedrum allowing you to learn new techniques without disturbing your neighbors. Among the free synth plugins available, Helm stands out for its user-friendly interface and feature-rich software synthesizer.

Metronome

Do you feel like your rhythm changes at times? It can speed up when you're excited or feel dragged at other times. You can improve your timing by using a metronome. At Soundbrenner, we create metronomes that use tactile cues, like vibrations, to help you stay on beat.

Check out our shop to learn more about our vibrating metronomes.

items.title: 5 common frustrations faced by self-taught pianists and how to overcome them
items.editorNotes: The article was rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original meaning and including all required links and images.
items.excerptBefore: What are common problems faced by self-taught pianists? We thought you might find this relatable. Check it out, possible resolutions to it.
items.excerptAfter: Explore common frustrations faced by self-taught pianists and discover effective strategies to overcome them. From tension in the forearms to poor rhythm, learn how to enhance your piano playing experience.
items.seoTitleBefore: 5 common frustrations faced by self-taught pianists and how to overcome them
items.seoTitleAfter: 5 Common Frustrations Faced by Self-Taught Pianists
items.seoDescriptionBefore: What are common problems faced by self-taught pianists? We thought you might find this relatable. Check it out, possible resolutions to it.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Learn about the common challenges self-taught pianists encounter and how to effectively overcome them. Improve your piano skills with practical tips and…
items.beforeBody:
The internet is a great place to teach yourself piano. You can watch YouTube videos, download chords, basic music theory, or even sheet music, to get started right away. While you’re at it, we thought you might find these common frustrations faced by other self-taught pianists relatable. The good news is we’ve also included ways to overcome these perils, which could be handy for your learning journey.

If you want to progress to more complicated pieces, however, we highly recommend finding a teacher to help you along the way!

1. Too much forearm tension

Playing the piano is a highly complex task, especially for a self-taught pianist. You have to get 10 fingers hitting the right notes, make sure you’re playing at the right tempo, and check that you’re not hitting the notes too heavily or too lightly… A lot is going on at the same time.

Inevitably, many self-taught pianists may find themselves playing with very tense arms as they try to focus on so many things. This causes the music to sound rigid instead of flowing easily. If not corrected early, poor hand positioning can become a habit that is really hard to change later on. To avoid this, begin paying attention to these small improvements that would make a huge difference in the long run:

  • Play with slightly curved fingers
  • Lower your wrists
  • Keep your fingers as close to the keyboard as possible. (Try not to lift your thumb up when you’re playing!)

2. Poor rhythm

As self-taught pianists rarely get feedback on their playing (such as that from a teacher), it comes as no surprise that some may end up playing pieces out of beat, without even realizing it! A teacher with adequate listening experience will spot right away what you’re not even aware of. There may be parts of a piece that you play faster or slower, and this could easily become your habitual way of playing if not corrected early.

To help yourself stay on beat, a metronome is always a good idea. There are traditional wooden metronomes, electronic ones, or even a watch like the Soundbrenner Core 2! Practicing with a metronome is a fantastic way to maintain your pace and develop better preciseness while playing.

Most metronomes allow you to follow its tick audibly or visually. The Soundbrenner Core 2 offers a different sensory cue — vibrations on your wrist. For musicians who prefer not to be distracted by clicks or lights, the Core 2 might just be the perfect tool for you.

3. Inability to sight-reading

Instead of learning to read sheet music, self-taught pianists may rely on playing by ear or synthesis. While there is nothing wrong with these approaches, there are many reasons why we think learning to read sheet music is highly beneficial!

Being able to read music can open up the doors for you as a self-taught pianist to learn a wider variety of musical styles, from classical to jazz, as you'll be familiar with the playing styles and techniques notated. It also gives you the ability to play with other musicians, as sheet music helps keep everyone in time. What's more, you can even compose your own music too.

Learning to read sheet music may seem daunting at first, but set a small goal to start reading for 5 minutes a day, and you'll start picking it up in no time!

4. Poor fingering

For self-taught pianists, fingering is often a process of trial and error to find which fingerings work best. The problem is, with poor fingering technique, you may not be able to reach your full potential in playing a piece smoothly.

With proper fingering instructions, you can use your hands as efficiently as possible and learn a smarter way of using your hands. Our advice is to get piano scores with fingerings and follow them strictly. Over time, you’ll learn how to play in a smarter was.

5. Ineffective practicing method

A better way to practice, to save time. Practice in smaller sections and repeat seven times. Repeat until you don’t have to think about it, and it feels like you can do it on autopilot. Instead of trying to master the entire piece at once, try compartmentalizing it into smaller sections. As a self-taught pianist, focus on learning and repeating one section at a time until you feel like you can play on autopilot. Chunking is very helpful for building muscle memory and accelerating the learning process.

items.title: Top 5 ways to practice polyrhythms
items.editorNotes: The article has been rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original content and links.
items.excerptBefore: In this article, we'll show you the top 5 ways to practice polyrhythms. Are you ready to become better at polyrhythms?
items.excerptAfter: Discover the top 5 effective methods to practice polyrhythms and enhance your musical skills.
items.seoTitleBefore: Top 5 ways to practice polyrhythms
items.seoTitleAfter: Top 5 Ways to Practice Polyrhythms
items.seoDescriptionBefore: In this article, we'll show you the top 5 ways to practice polyrhythms. Are you ready to become better at polyrhythms?
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Learn effective techniques to master polyrhythms with our top 5 practice methods. Improve your rhythm skills today!
items.beforeBody:
Polyrhythms are rather easy in theory, but might turn out tricky when you actually try them out yourself. To get the basics straight, we suggest that you check this article out. In this article, we'll show you the top 5 ways to practice polyrhythms. Are you ready to become better at polyrhythms?

 

1. Break it down

 

Let’s take a look at a 3:2 polyrhythm example.

 

While you might think that looks complicated, it’s actually pretty simple. First, you have to isolate one rhythmic cell. Note that you don’t have to pay attention to the whole note for now.

 

Then, since 3 and 2 do not have any common factors other than 1, we have to multiply them.

 

2 × 3 = 6

 

We then put this information in a grid:

 

Now, use your right hand and tap on any surface while counting until 6. Continue counting, but skip the taps in-between and tap only on 1, 3, and 5. There goes your triplet.

 

Let’s do the same for the two eighth notes. First, tap, and count until 6, and then tap only on 1 and 4 while still counting.

 

The tricky part is when you put them together. Look at the grid above, count until 6, and tap your right hand according to the top line and the left to the bottom. If you can, say ta when you tap the triplet, and then when you tap the two eighth notes to really hear the difference. 

 

Let’s now take a look at another example in 4:3.

 

Again, let’s isolate a cell:

 

Now, you might wonder, “Why did we cut the sextuplet in half”? Simply because 8 sixteenth notes play during the sextuplet, and 8:6 is the same as 4:3. 

 

Again, 4 and 3 do not have a common factor other than 1, so we multiply them.

 

4 × 3 = 12

 

We transfer it on a grid:

 

Now, all you have to do is repeat the steps from above, only counting until 12 this time!

 

2. Practice with a metronome

 

Struggling to get it right? No worries, the metronome is here to help. Download our free metronome app (available for iOS and Android) and start slow, at 60 BPM. Set the metronome at 12/8 to have all the counts. All you have to do is look at the grid and tap on the beats marked with an x.

 

Alternatively, you can also assign one of the layers to the metronome: if it’s 2 eighth notes or a duplet, set the metronome at 2/4, if it’s 3 eighth notes or a triplet, set the metronome at 3/4, and so on. Perform the other layer on your instrument or by clapping your hands.

 

3. Use your DAW's step sequencer

 

Remember that MIDI piano roll grid in your DAW? It’s actually a very useful tool to practice polyrhythms. Set the correct time signature and fill the grid the same way as above. For best results, use a drum kit to really hear the difference between the two contrasting rhythms. Here is an example of the 4:3 polyrhythm:

 

4. Add accents

 

Try this only if you have mastered the steps above. Let’s go back to our 3:2 example. Tap the triplets with your right hand and the eighth notes with your left. Now add an accent on the first eighth of the triplet and keep tapping. Then add the accent on the second, and finally the third. Still easy?

 

Here is the ultimate challenge. Combine all three, so that the accent is on the first eighth of the first triplet, the second eighth of the second triplet, and the third eighth of the third triplet. Additionally, you can add accents for the left hand too.

 

5. Use words

 

Chances are, you aren’t going to remember when exactly fell your ta when you practiced those polyrhythms. A great memorizing technique involves the use of simple words. If there are two notes in a beat, you can use pa-sta, three spa-ghe-tti, four ma-ca-ro-ni, and five ca-la-ma-ra-ta. You will achieve proficiency in music as well as pasta types in time.

 

Maybe one of your school music teachers used the Takadimi system. It’s an alternative way to learn and pronounce rhythm using different syllables. Here is a guide, but we would only recommend using it if you have used Takadimi in the past.

 

 

Anja Drozdova is a Swiss-Russian musician and music teacher. She focuses on finding creative ways to engage students during piano and music theory lessons by using different mediums such as technology and visual arts. Anja also writes electronic pop music under the artist name Mlkshk. Her music is inspired by everyday life, scenes from childhood, and imaginary places, shapes and colors.

items.title: Best online resources to brush up on your music theory
items.editorNotes: Ensure all links and images are correctly referenced and functional.
items.excerptBefore: If you are looking to expand your knowledge on music theory, here are a few suggestions for free online resources.
items.excerptAfter: Explore the best online resources to enhance your music theory knowledge and rhythm skills, from interactive lessons to structured courses.
items.seoTitleBefore: Best online resources to brush up on your music theory
items.seoTitleAfter: Best Online Resources for Music Theory
items.seoDescriptionBefore: If you are looking to expand your knowledge on music theory, here are a few suggestions for free online resources.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover top online platforms and tools to improve your music theory understanding and rhythm skills. Learn from experts and practice effectively.
items.beforeBody:
The abundance of online resources for music practice really leaves us with no excuse not to practice anytime and anywhere. Here are some online resources that you can use to learn all about music theory and rhythm.

1. Musictheory.net

If you have little to no knowledge of the language of music, you need to check out musictheory.net. Learn everything from note durations, measures, and time signatures to chord progressions and Neapolitan chords. They use animation, highlights, and a clean presentation style to make their online music lessons very clear and intuitive, especially for visual learners.

(Lesson on Staff, Clefs and Ledger lines on musictheory.net)

We suggest using a tablet or laptop when viewing this content. The best part is that it's free, and they have a pop-up interactive piano!

2. Coursera.org

If you’re into structured syllabi, quizzes, and exams, then Fundamentals of Music Theory on Coursera is the one for you. You’ll have doctorate-level instructors with degrees from prestigious music colleges.

(Week 1 syllabus of Fundamentals of Theory)

3. Rhythm specific learning: Soundbrenner Learn

Part of our new app subscription service, Soundbrenner Plus, includes a section for videos and articles dedicated to mastering rhythm.

We’ve produced bite-sized online music video lessons featuring rhythm instructors from different musical backgrounds. Choose from four categories of learning, including ‘Rhythm Exercises’ packed with drills and rhythmic warmups to put theory into practice.

Soundbrenner Plus with learn, song library and deals page

‘Groove’ goes in depth on the how and why a specific genre holds a specific rhythm - from grooves of jazz, rock to Afro-cuban music. Two other sections include ‘Musicality and Practice’ and ‘Fundamentals of Rhythm’.

If you would like more information, simply download our app for a free trial of Soundbrenner Plus.

iOS or Android

4. YouTuber: Adam Neely

YouTube-famous online music educator Adam Neely makes music theory cool and interesting. He dissects things like 7/11 polyrhythms, microtonal lo-fi hip hop, jazz, and even j-pop. Being a multi-instrumentalist, his video essays, lessons, and vlogs give a fresh perspective on music and music theory.

(Adam Neely teaching about Brazilian bossa nova)

5. Ear training app interval

Just like playing the piano or improving your vocal chain, you can train your ear and play melody by ear. Recognizing chord progressions by ear can help you be more creative with writing songs. You can easily steer yourself away or near the common progressions through online music training.

Although most of these apps may have interfaces that came from the 90s, we believe that it is helpful for both musicians and engineers alike.

  • Tenuto
  • Teoria
  • Earmaster

6. Music theory examples by women

This is a database of excerpts and complete musical compositions by women composers. The music is categorized by theoretical concepts for use in online music education.

Whether you’re a teacher looking for some helpful resources to use in the classroom, a musician doing some concert planning, or just finding the next skill to get better in - this is the page for you-music theory

(Women in music)

7. Music theory academy

From structure, chords, and tonality to composing, you can find FREE online music theory lessons throughout this website. They also give out music theory quizzes and worksheets to help you test your music theory.

Fairly advanced online music musicians often find it difficult to find more free resources, but Music Theory Academy has advanced topics including Picardy third and Ostinato. - music theory academy

6. Music theory examples by women

This is a database of excerpts and complete musical compositions by women composers. The music is categorized by theoretical concepts for use in online music education.

Whether you’re a teacher looking for helpful resources to use in the classroom, a musician planning a concert, or just seeking to improve your skills, this is the page for you: music theory examples by women.

(Women in music)

7. Music theory academy

From structure, chords, and tonality to composing, you can find free online music theory lessons throughout this website. They also provide music theory quizzes and worksheets to help you test your knowledge.

Advanced musicians often find it difficult to locate free resources, but Music Theory Academy offers advanced topics including Picardy third and Ostinato. Check out Music Theory Academy.

items.title: The ultimate beginner’s guide to recording drums
How to count beats in music: 3 easy steps
items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while preserving original content and links.
items.excerptBefore: It's important to figure out how many beats there are in a piece before you start playing it. In this article, we'll show you the three easy steps you can take in learning how to count beats in music.
items.excerptAfter: Learn how to count beats in music with these three easy steps, including understanding time signatures, note lengths, and practical exercises.
items.seoTitleBefore: How to count beats in music: 3 easy steps
items.seoTitleAfter: How to Count Beats in Music: 3 Easy Steps
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Before playing a piece, it’s important to know how many beats it has. This article covers three easy steps to count beats in music.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover three simple steps to effectively count beats in music, including understanding time signatures and note lengths. Perfect for beginners!
items.beforeBody:
It's important to figure out how many beats there are in a piece before you start playing it. But don’t worry, you'll probably only have to count up to four! In this article, we'll show you the three easy steps you can take in learning how to count beats in music.

P.S. Beginners, not too sure what the difference between rhythms and beats are? We've got you covered ;)

1. Understand time signatures

Ever paid attention to the two little numbers right after the clef? The top number basically tells you how many, while the bottom number tells of what. The top number can be anything greater than or equal to 2, while the top number is usually 2, 4, or 8, and represents the value of the beat. Here is a little chart:

For example, if you have a 3/2 time signature, it means that there are 3 half notes in a bar. If you have 3/4, there are 3 quarter notes in a bar. Finally, if you see 3/8, it means there are 3 eighth notes in a bar. It does go past eight; the next bottom numbers are 16, 32, 64, etc., but they are far less common.

Sometimes, you will see a C or a C with a vertical line over it instead of numbers. They are also time signatures: C means 4/4 and C with the line over it means 2/2, they are just symbols from a few centuries ago but are still widely used in sheet music.

2. Know the lengths of the notes

Let’s go back to 3/4. If that’s the time signature of your piece, you will find 3 quarter notes in each bar. Not literal 3 quarter notes, but the total value of the notes within the bar should be equal to 3 quarter notes. Here is an example of a piece in 3/4:

Of course, to fully understand how to count beats in music, and knowing how many beats there are in a piece, you should be familiar with the lengths of notes and rests. These two diagrams are a good place to start:

The best way to get the number of beats is to find and circle them directly on the piece. Here is an example, with 4 quarter notes in each bar:

Because the time signature is 4/4, the beat (1) represents the quarter note. Hence, a whole note has four beats and an eighth note is only the half of a beat.

Finally, one last example in 6/8, where we should have 6 eighth notes per bar:

There is a little catch. In 6/8, the beat is not the eighth note, but rather a dotted quarter note. That is because 6/8 is a compound meter, along with 9/8 and 12/8, and we feel the beats per group of 3 eighth notes:

3. Clap and use your voice

As long as you figure out the number of beats and the notes they fall on, you can practice by clapping the beat and using your voice for the notes. You can use any syllable you want, though it’s common to use ta. If we go back to 4/4, a whole note becomes ta-a-a-a and a half note just ta-a. Here is an example, with the beats marked with an x:

If clapping and using your voice is too much coordination for now, you can always use a metronome instead of clapping. Our free metronome app is available for Android and iOS.

In the case you are still unsure about how many beats there are, listen to the piece on your favorite streaming platform and try following along with the sheet music!



Anja Drozdova is a Swiss-Russian musician and music teacher. She focuses on finding creative ways to engage students during piano and music theory lessons by using different mediums such as technology and visual arts. Anja also writes electronic pop music under the artist name Mlkshk. Her music is inspired by everyday life, scenes from childhood, and imaginary places, shapes, and colors.

Finger stretches for guitar players to avoid injuries?
items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while preserving factual content and required links/images.
items.excerptBefore: Finger stretches for guitar players are crucial to maintain muscle elasticity and avoid injury. Here are four exercises to try out.
items.excerptAfter: Discover essential finger stretches for guitar players to prevent injuries and enhance flexibility. Learn effective exercises to keep your hands healthy while playing.
items.seoTitleBefore: Finger stretches for guitar players to avoid injuries?
items.seoTitleAfter: Finger Stretches for Guitar Players to Avoid Injuries
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Finger stretches for guitar players are crucial to maintain muscle elasticity and avoid injury. Here are four exercises to try out.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore essential finger stretches for guitar players to prevent injuries and improve flexibility. Discover effective exercises to keep your hands healthy…
items.beforeBody:
Playing guitar is a beautiful way of expressing yourself and letting your creativity flow. But have you ever considered what parts of your body it’s the hardest on? That’s right, your fingers and hands.

It’s easy to get excited when first learning the art of playing guitar and over-do yourself. The Metronome app by Soundbrenner is an invaluable resource for musicians with built-in reminders, practice tracking, and much more! Remember that while some soreness is a natural part of any new activity, pain should never be ignored.

Our top 4 stretching exercises for guitarists

Finger stretches for guitar players are a must to retain muscle tone and increase elasticity and flexibility. Just as a ballerina must regularly stretch and practice staying in optimal performance condition, achy shoulders and throbbing fingers will ultimately affect your jamming sessions negatively.

Show your hands the love they deserve for allowing you to practice your craft by trying these finger stretches for guitar players:

1. Massage it out

Hold one finger at a time with your thumb and index finger. Gently pull forward while rotating your finger back and forth. This gentle massage will help with stiffness and make your fingers supple. Massage all fingers one by one, taking care to exert moderate pressure and pull.

2. The Buddha stretch

We’ve named this one the Buddha stretch because of its resemblance and Zen-like relaxation it gives your achy fingers and side tendons!

Join palms flat in front of your chest and slowly push them together while descending them down. Taking care to keep palms joined, hold the position for 15 seconds. Then gently roll your wrist so that your fingers point towards the ground. If you feel pain, don’t extend your hands any further.

3. The stop sign

Extend your arm in front of you as you would stop a car or taxi. Now interlock the other hand's fingers with your extended hand and pull backward till you feel them slightly stretch. Hold the position for half a minute before repeating it for your other hand.

These stretches will not only soothe your hands, but your shoulders and back as well.

4. Shake it out!

Stand in a comfortable spot and take a few deep breaths to relax your body and muscles completely. Hang your arms nice and loose at the sides. Shake your hands and let your fingers fly about, keeping your elbows completely relaxed.

This super simple shake re-energizes your fingers before and after your practice sessions. It gets your blood pumping again and helps with the delayed onset of soreness.

Get your groove on, minus the pain!

Playing guitar is all about how you refine your skill through practice. Make sure to check out these great practice tips for beginners. Having the right gear, such as the Soundbrenner Core 2, can elevate your groove sessions and help you make the most of your time.
